entry.title: SAP Business Client 8.10
entry.content:
Today we are happy to launch SAP Business Client 8.10, together with SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 .

This release is fully downward compatible with former releases. The following new features have been implemented:

 

Horizon Theming:

Morning Horizon Theme
SAP Business Client 8.10 offers Horizon theming, including Morning Horizon, Evening Horizon and HC-variants. Additionally, Custom Theming for Horizon themes is available for On-Premise and Cloud systems.

Joule : 

Together with SAP GUI for Windows 8.10, the SAP Business Client allows users to use Joule for SAP GUI for Windows transactions, enabling Joule to access the current transaction and apply Frontend Actions.

See more: Joule meets Classic UI

Edge Browser Extensions: 
With release 8.10, the SAP Business Client is open to Edge browser extensions, allowing companies to enhance their SAP Business Client experience.

Admin Command Line Toolset: 
This toolset allows an administrator to modify and change data and client behaviour via command line and possibly batch.

Index, New Tab and Service Map page in WPF: 
Formerly HTML-based internal pages are now recreated in WPF for easier theming and control.

Tab Navigation via JS:
Via an extra API in the WindowExternal-object, it's possible to create and manage IB-navigated tabs in the Fiori Launchpad via Javascript code.

Quicklaunch improvements:
The SAP Business Client 8.10 provides an improved Quicklaunch including autocomplete, fuzzy search, share, zoom and add favorite functionalities.

Enhanced Back and Forward Navigation UI: 
Back and Forward navigation buttons are available and allow access to the list of past navigation targets.

Restore Mechanism: 
Like in a standalone web browser, in case of an unlikely crash or simply after logon, it's possible to restore the tabs from the former session.

System Selection Screen Improvements: 
Including advanced filtering across workspaces and non-alphabetic ordering.

And Many More ...

 

For full usage of SAP GUI for Windows within SAP Business Client 8.10, SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 is required.

For details how to make use of all this new functionality, please see SAP Help Portal - SAP Business Client 8.10 .

Download

entry.title: The Fourth Way to Go Offline in UI5 (And Why the Service Worker Stays Dumb)
entry.content:
Written up after attending "Offline capabilities in UI5 – Let's discuss" at UI5Con, run by Viktor Sperling and Lars Kissel from SAP's UI5 team as an open discussion rather than a one-way talk. The app described below is a real, shipped project — a handheld scanning app for warehouse/inventory operations — with client-identifying details deliberately left out.

Three ideas, three shapes of "offline"

The session's core insight, stated up front, was that "offline" isn't one problem — it has a shape, and the right architecture depends on that shape. They presented three ideas, each solving a distinct scenario:

  • Idea 1 — OData V4 Model as Change Buffer. v4.ODataModel holds changes locally (updateGroupId), disables actions that can't succeed offline, submits once back online (submitBatch). Connectivity is actively probed — a fetch/AbortController ping, not just navigator.onLine. Scenario: a commuter on a train, signal drops between two stations. Duration: seconds to minutes.
  • Idea 2 — Service Worker as OData Proxy. The service worker becomes an OData-aware proxy: it parses $batch and read requests, resolves query options, keeps its own IndexedDB store and changelog, and rebuilds a combined $batch on reconnect. Scenario: a field worker visiting customers, connection comes and goes. Duration: minutes to hours.
  • Idea 3 — JSONModel with Explicit Sync. An explicit download → work → submit workflow. JSONModel as the UI model, a v4.ODataModel downloads the required entity sets up front, changes go into a changelog, submitted via Context#setProperty/#delete. Scenario: a traveler on a flight, working for hours in airplane mode. Duration: hours to days.

Idea 1 is elegant if you can stay inside the OData V4 model's own change-tracking. Idea 3 matches the shape of what we needed almost exactly — an explicit, user-initiated offline session, not an accidental one.

Idea 2 is the one worth pausing on, because it's easy to misread it as "just cache the OData responses." It doesn't. It builds its own IndexedDB store and changelog inside the service worker, and re-implements a slice of the OData protocol there — parsing $batch payloads, matching query options against locally held entity sets, replaying a changelog into a fresh $batch on reconnect. That's a genuinely more sophisticated design than an HTTP cache, and it's also exactly why it didn't fit our case: it means OData-protocol knowledge and business logic now live inside a service worker — a context that's already awkward to debug, awkward to unit test, and now also carries backend-shaped logic.

The session closed with a "Pain Points" slide worth quoting directly, because it names this tension independent of any particular implementation:

Back-end logic in the client — understanding the OData protocol, $filter options that quickly become complex, resolving navigation properties locally, duplicating business logic, OData Actions, server-side calculated fields.

Conflicts at sync time — concurrent modifications or business-rule violations, and expired authentication or session tokens, refresh before sync.

That first point is the crux of this whole post: the problem isn't "service workers shouldn't hold data." The problem is putting protocol-aware business logic inside a service worker specifically. The approach below solves the same problem idea 2 solves — resilience across a flaky connection — without paying that price.

Our shape of offline

Before the architecture, the shape of the problem, because it explains every choice below:

  • Runs on ruggedized handheld scanners with genuinely intermittent connectivity — sometimes hours without a signal, not a brief tunnel dip.
  • Scans in bulk via RFID — hundreds of local mutations in a single session before anything needs to reach a server.
  • Submits as a batch at the end of a session, not request-per-scan — the backend should never see a half-finished state.

That's squarely "spontaneous, business-logic-heavy, shared" territory — borrowing the session's own three-axis framing (Preparation: predictable ↔ spontaneous; Interaction complexity: simple form ↔ business logic; Conflict sensitivity: exclusive ↔ shared) — which rules out idea 1 immediately and makes idea 2's in-worker protocol logic feel like the wrong place to carry that weight.

A fourth option: two layers, two jobs

The architecture splits cleanly into two layers that don't know about each other's internals.

Two isolated layers on the device. The service worker never touches OData or $batch; the JSON model/IndexedDB layer owns all sync logic and only reaches the backend at two named moments — a fetch at the start of a session, a $batch submit at the end.

Layer 1 — the shell, and only the shell

The service worker knows nothing about OData, nothing about business entities. Its fetch strategy has three branches:

  • Skip — OData entity sets, lookup endpoints, $batch: always straight to the network, never cached. Stale business data is worse than a failed fetch, and offline resilience for that data lives entirely in the layer below.
  • Network-first with a timeout race — our own app resources (JS/CSS/XML/i18n): online gets fresh content plus a cache update, offline or a timeout falls back to cache. We deliberately avoid navigator.onLine — it lies in both directions. Racing a real fetch() against a timeout is symmetrically correct for "no network" and "server unreachable" alike.
  • Cache-first — third-party/library resources that rarely change.
const NETWORK_TIMEOUT_MS = 2000;

function rejectAfter(ms) {
  return new Promise((_, reject) =>
    setTimeout(() => reject(new Error("SW network-timeout")), ms)
  );
}

async function networkFirst(request) {
  const cache = await caches.open(CACHE_NAME);
  try {
    const response = await Promise.race([fetch(request), rejectAfter(NETWORK_TIMEOUT_MS)]);
    if (isCacheable(response)) cache.put(request, response.clone());
    return response;
  } catch {
    const cached = await cache.match(request, { ignoreSearch: true });
    if (cached) return cached;
    throw new Error("no network, no cache");
  }
}

One gotcha worth flagging for anyone building this themselves: cross-origin <script src> loads — like the UI5 runtime, loaded no-cors — come back as opaque responses. response.ok is false even on success, but the body is perfectly cacheable. Skip that check and your library bundles silently never get cached.

Loading UI5 itself deserves its own paragraph. Our bootstrap script picks its UI5 source depending on where it's running: an on-premise deployment loads sap-ui-core.js from the same server (no internet dependency at all), local dev loads from the public SAPUI5 CDN. "Offline-first" starts at the very first <script> tag — if you fetch the UI5 runtime itself from a CDN, your app is, by definition, not offline-capable anywhere without internet access. Those two sources produce different response types (same-origin vs. cross-origin no-cors), which is exactly why the opaque check above matters — without it, cache-first would silently never cache the UI5 runtime in the on-premise case.

Also worth knowing: the service worker doesn't register at all when the app runs inside a Fiori Launchpad — more on why below.

Layer 2 — data, entirely in ordinary application code

IndexedDB is the source of truth; the JSON model is loaded from it on boot and reloaded after every mutation. OData traffic isn't scattered through the app — it happens at exactly two named operations:

  1. Fetch — pull existing records from the backend when online, including a bulk status update so colleagues can see something's been picked up.
  2. Submit — at the end of a session, one $batch per document with everything — header update, item/serial changes — inside a single changeSet, so the backend never sees a half-written state.
private _queueHeaderUpdate(record: Order): void {
  this.model.update(getHeaderPath(record.id), {
    status: "3",
    processedBy: record.processedBy,
    processedAt: toBackendTimestamp()
  }, { merge: true, groupId: BATCH_GROUP, changeSetId: CHANGE_SET });
}

private _submitBatch(): Promise<void> {
  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    this.model.submitChanges({
      groupId: BATCH_GROUP,
      success: (data) => this._hasError(data) ? reject(data) : resolve(),
      error: reject
    });
  });
}

After a successful sync, the server's response is merged back into the local record — fields the server owns get overwritten, purely local UI state stays as-is.

This is, in workflow terms, closest to idea 3 — download, work, submit — but hardened with a persistent IndexedDB layer underneath instead of a purely in-memory JSONModel, so a page refresh mid-session doesn't throw everything away.

The bit I'm not proud to admit: session expiry

An architecture built around hours of offline work immediately raises a question: what happens to the session/XSRF token when you come back online? Honest answer: we don't have a good one yet.

  • There's no custom 401/403 detection or re-login flow anywhere in the app. Every OData call leans entirely on the OData V2 model's default XSRF fetch/retry behavior.
  • The batch-submit code only checks HTTP status codes (>= 400) to decide whether a sync succeeded — it doesn't distinguish "the server rejected this data" from "the session expired, retry after a token refresh."
  • Practical consequence: if a device has been offline for a day and the underlying session has since expired, the first sync batch after reconnecting fails as a generic error, not a recognizable "please log in again."

Reassurance, not excuse. This isn't a shortcoming unique to this approach. The session's own Pain Points slide names it directly: "expired authentication or session tokens — refresh before sync." Even SAP's own UI5 team presents this as an open problem across all three of their ideas, not something any of them has solved. That took some pressure off having to invent something clever here — naming it honestly is enough.

This isn't a showstopper in practice (logging back in on the device always fixes it), but it is a real gap: this architecture solves the transport problem, not the identity problem. "Offline-first" here does not mean "session-independent." You'd need to layer explicit 401 detection with a clear re-auth prompt on top if that matters for your case.

How the cache invalidates itself

Every build — production or local dev start — stamps a fresh build timestamp into a small generated file, imported into the service worker via importScripts. The service worker script itself never changes between builds; only that one value does.

self.BUILD_VERSION = "1737012345678"; // stamped per build

const CACHE_NAME = "app-v" + self.BUILD_VERSION;

self.addEventListener("activate", (event) => {
  event.waitUntil(
    caches.keys().then((keys) =>
      Promise.all(
        keys.filter((k) => k.startsWith("app-v") && k !== CACHE_NAME)
            .map((k) => caches.delete(k))
      )
    )
  );
});

No semver, no manual version bump to forget — a timestamp per build means the cache invalidates itself automatically on every build/start. Worth noting separately: the app's manifest version is a static marker, unrelated to this cache version or the OData service version — a deliberate decoupling, since it's tempting to conflate all three kinds of "version."

The Fiori Launchpad has its own appetite for the network

A natural question: could this run inside a Fiori Launchpad instead of standalone? In our case, no — and it's worth explaining why, because the reason generalizes.

A Launchpad is itself an application full of network-dependent shell services that have nothing to do with the app you're hosting inside it: personalization (tile layout, saved filters), intent/navigation resolution against a catalog, notifications, enterprise search, usage tracking, user preferences and theming. None of that is "your app" — you don't control it as an app builder, and none of it is designed to gracefully degrade without a network. That's exactly why registering a custom service worker under a Launchpad route conflicts with the shell: it already has its own lifecycle and caching assumptions, and a second service worker layered on top produces, at best, inconsistent behavior. Practically, our app runs standalone via a direct deep link, not as a Launchpad tile.

Speculative — a wish, not a feature. An officially supported "shell offline mode" would let an individual app register its own service worker scoped strictly to its own sub-path — the shell owning the root scope — and would let shell services degrade gracefully (personalization/notifications/search no-op instead of blocking) rather than all-or-nothing. To be clear, I'm not aware this exists today; it's the question this project left open, and a good one to put back to the community.

What about Fiori Elements and OData V4?

Also raised at the session: Fiori Elements with V4 bindings makes offline hard. I think that's right, and it's worth explaining why rather than just asserting it.

Fiori Elements is metadata- and annotation-driven: value helps, filters, side effects after an edit, and draft handling are, at their core, live interactions with the OData V4 service — they're not designed to be replayed from a local cache. sap.ui.model.odata.v4.ODataModel does keep an in-memory cache of already-loaded data, but that's request-level caching within a session, not a persistent offline store with conflict resolution the way a dedicated IndexedDB layer is. Historically, SAP's "real" offline-OData story has lived more at the native/mobile SDK level than in-browser Fiori Elements — worth double-checking the current state of that, since this area moves.

Practically, the options that hold up today, roughly in order of "how much Fiori Elements you keep":

  1. Limit offline to read-mostly reference data (cache value-help lists locally), keep write flows online.
  2. Freestyle islands inside an otherwise Fiori-Elements-driven app for exactly the screens that need to work offline.
  3. Go fully freestyle, as we did — you lose FE's automation (annotations → UI), but gain full control over what lives locally.

Fiori Elements optimizes for speed and consistency while you're online; the moment offline becomes a hard requirement, you're trading away precisely the automation that makes FE valuable — and freestyle starts looking attractive again.

Lessons that cost us a debugging session

  • Silent cache misses on lazy-loaded fragments. Dialogs that only load at runtime via Fragment.load failed silently if they weren't explicitly named in the pre-cache list — an empty dialog, no error — and the symptom only ever showed up under genuine offline testing, never in normal dev with a network.
  • navigator.onLine lies. It's true whenever a network interface exists, not when a server is reachable, and false isn't reliable either. A race between a real fetch() and a timeout is symmetrically correct in both directions.
  • Two UI5 sources, two response types. The same bootstrap code sometimes loads UI5 same-origin, sometimes cross-origin/no-cors — normal vs. opaque responses — and a cacheability check that only looks at response.ok silently misses the second case.
  • A service worker and a Fiori Launchpad shell don't mix. Registering a custom SW under a Launchpad route caused conflicts with the shell's own layer; registration had to be explicitly excluded for those routes.
  • Deliberately no IndexedDB schema migration. On a breaking model change, we chose a new database name over a migration path — old local data is simply ignored, not converted. For a small, known fleet of devices carrying mostly in-transit data, that's a cheaper, safer call than maintaining migration code.
  • Session expiry isn't solved for free by any of this — see above. Worth saying explicitly so the takeaway isn't "offline-first means never thinking about sessions again."

When this pattern fits — and when it doesn't

This isn't "the best option" — it's the option that fits a specific shape of app. The session's own "Technical Aspects" slide offers a genuinely useful way to reason about that shape: three axes — Preparation (predictable ↔ spontaneous), Interaction complexity (simple form ↔ business logic), and Conflict sensitivity (exclusive ↔ shared). Our app sits firmly at the right-hand end of every axis — unpredictable offline moments, non-trivial document logic, multiple colleagues touching the same stock. That's exactly why idea 1, built for predictable and simple cases, wasn't a fit.

Fits well:

  • Apps where users mutate a lot before anything needs to reach a server (scan sessions, inspections, field forms) — the win is decoupling "mutating" from "submitting."
  • Longer, unpredictable offline periods (minutes to hours), not just a brief tunnel dip.
  • Business logic that doesn't fit neatly into a generic entity cache — merges, batch/changeSet semantics, status derived from other fields, locally created records that only get a server ID at sync time.
  • A limited, known set of devices/users, where "no automatic IndexedDB migration" and "manual session recovery" are acceptable trade-offs.

Fits less well:

  • Apps where nearly every action needs a direct server round-trip anyway (real-time collaborative, prices/stock that must be correct right now).
  • Short, second-scale, unprepared offline blips: idea 1 is probably cheaper to build and maintain — you get most of the resilience for free from the model itself.
  • Many concurrent users editing the same records ("shared" on the conflict-sensitivity axis): this pattern solves "one device, local, sync later," not multi-user conflict resolution. Look at CRDTs, optimistic locking with real conflict UI, or just staying online with a good loading state instead.
  • Teams that would rather lean on a standardized offline framework than maintain a custom SW + IndexedDB layer.

A short aside: why not Cordova, Capacitor, or a native SDK?

A fair question: why a PWA/service-worker approach, and not a hybrid or native app that's offline-capable by default? First, a framing correction: a service worker isn't an experimental bet — it's a mature, broadly supported web standard. This isn't "proven vs. experimental," it's "proven vs. proven."

  • Apache Cordova (formerly PhoneGap) wraps a web app in a native webview shell with plugin access to device APIs. Still an active Apache project, but development velocity has been low for years — much of the hybrid ecosystem has moved toward Capacitor. Worth checking current release cadence and plugin health before repeating that as fact, since it dates quickly.
  • Capacitor (Ionic) is the de facto successor for "web app in a native shell" — a similar model, more actively maintained, a more modern plugin architecture.
  • Native, vendor-specific offline SDKs often ship a ready-made offline layer with built-in conflict resolution — at the cost of vendor lock-in: a proprietary data model/SDK, lower portability, and the loss of "it's just a web app, patch with a new build."

We stayed on web/PWA for three reasons: it's built on proven, stable technology rather than a moving target; a new build is a set of static files picked up by the service worker on the next online load — no app-store review, no MDM rollout cycle per patch; and the sync logic is our own readable code against a standard OData service, not a proprietary SDK locking us to one vendor.

Where this leaves things

None of this is a silver bullet, and it isn't meant to replace the session's three ideas — it's meant to sit alongside them as a fourth shape for a fourth kind of problem. If your offline story is short and unprepared, idea 1 will get you further with less code. If you want the app itself to stay entirely unaware of offline handling, idea 2's service-worker-as-proxy is a genuinely clever answer, provided you're comfortable carrying protocol logic in that layer. If your offline sessions are long, deliberate, and business-logic-heavy — ours were — this is the shape that held up.

Thanks to Viktor and Lars for running the session as a discussion instead of a monologue — it's the reason this idea exists in writable form at all. Curious how others have handled the session-expiry gap in particular — that felt like the one open thread nobody in the room had a clean answer for.

entry.title: Freight Order Quantity Analysis
entry.content:
In SAP Transportation Management in transportation execution and operational visibility scenarios. In one weekly review, the business team wanted to understand whether transportation volume was evenly distributed across carriers.

The first comparison was based mainly on the number of completed Freight Orders. At first glance, two carriers looked almost equal because their Freight Order counts were close. However, when we reviewed the transported gross weight and gross volume, we found that the actual workload was not the same.

For confidentiality, I have used representative carrier names and adjusted figures. The scenario is based on the type of operational review I have worked on, but the numbers are not production values.
Accessing the App the Freight Order Quantity Analysis app can be accessed from the SAP Fiori launchpad.

  1. Open the SAP Fiori launchpad.
  2. Search for Freight Order Quantity Analysis.
  3. Open the app with Fiori app ID F2676.
  4. From the overview page, select the relevant analytical card or data point.
  5. Navigate to the detailed view to analyze the Freight Orders behind the selected KPI.

The overview page provides different analytical cards, such as gross weight, gross volume, quantity distribution, carrier quantity analysis, shipper quantity analysis, ship to party quantity analysis, transportation mode and utilization.


Figure 1: Freight Order Quantity Analysis overview page - Fiori app F2676

Filters Used for the Analysis from the overview page, I selected the Carrier Quantity Analysis card and opened the detailed view. For the analysis, I used the following filter and display selections:

  • Transportation Mode Category: Road
  • Destination Country/Region: United States
  • View By: Carrier
  • Measure: Gross Weight
  • Display Weight Unit: Metric Tons
  • Display Volume Unit: Cubic Metres
  • Display Quantity Unit: Each
  • Completion Period: Selected completion weeks within the available current and previous month period

The standard app focuses on completed Freight Orders for the current and previous month. Because of this, I used the Completion Year/Week field to compare weekly movement trends.

These fields helped to compare the number of Freight Orders against the actual transportation volume.


Figure 2: Detailed view with carrier, transportation mode, units and completion week filters

Representative Carrier Comparison, The original business question was simple: Which carrier handled more work this week?
If we looked only at the number of Freight Orders, Carrier A appeared to have handled slightly more work than Carrier B.

Measure

Carrier A

Carrier B

Completed Freight Orders

42

39

Gross Weight

840 metric tons

1,260 metric tons

Gross Volume

1,960 m³

2,450 m³

Based on Freight Order count alone, Carrier A looked slightly higher because it completed 42 Freight Orders compared with Carrier B’s 39 Freight Orders. However, the quantity analysis showed a different story. Carrier B completed three fewer Freight Orders, but transported approximately 50% more gross weight. Carrier B also transported higher gross volume.
This finding changed the discussion. Carrier A had more documents, but Carrier B had a higher actual transportation workload. Carrier B was handling fewer but heavier movements, while Carrier A was handling a larger number of smaller shipments.

The main finding was that Freight Order count should not be used as the only measure of carrier workload. In this example, the business initially assumed that Carrier A had a slightly higher workload because it had more completed Freight Orders. After reviewing gross weight and gross volume, we could see that Carrier B had consumed more transportation capacity.
This was useful for three reasons:
1. It gave the team a better view of actual carrier workload.
2. It supported a more meaningful capacity discussion with carriers.
3. It helped explain why carrier utilization may look different from the document count.

The app also helped identify weekly movement patterns. When I changed the view to Completion Week, I could see the week in which Carrier B’s transported weight increased. This made it easier to review the underlying Freight Orders and understand what caused the change. Drilling Down into the Freight Orders after identifying the increase for Carrier B, I reviewed the detailed table and checked the Freight Orders behind the result.
Compared the following information:

  • Source and destination locations
  • Gross weight and volume
  • Number of containers
  • Transportation mode
  • Freight Order completion date
  • Carrier assignment

The list showed that Carrier B had handled several high weight movements to the same destination region during that week. This explained why the gross weight was higher even though the Freight Order count was lower. This is the part I found most useful. The overview helped identify the trend and the detailed view helped explain the reason behind it.


Figure 3: Carrier comparison showing Freight Order count, gross weight, and gross volume

It is also important to understand that this analysis is based on completed Freight Orders. Freight Orders that are still being planned or executed may not be represented in the same way. This can explain differences between the application and another operational report that includes planned or in execution documents.
From a technical perspective, the app uses the C_FrtOrdQuantityQuery CDS view. The C_FRTORDQUANTITYQUERY_CDS OData service must be activated in the SAP Gateway system. Users also need the required SAP Transportation Management and SAP Fiori authorizations to access the app and view the analytical data. No custom development was required for this analysis. The key requirements were correct Freight Order data, the required authorizations and activation of the supporting analytical service.

Conclusion
The Freight Order Quantity Analysis app gives better insight into transportation volumes and supports more informed decision making. It helps teams understand not just how many Freight Orders are moving, but also how much business volume is being executed. The app may look simple at first, but when used together with operational knowledge, it can provide useful insights that are not always visible from standard Freight Order reports.

Reference: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/612fbe25aed64068a7aca8e8e8339de1.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com

entry.title: Advanced VizFrame Customization in SAPUI5 OVP Applications
entry.content:
Advanced VizFrame Customization in SAPUI5 OVP Applications

Introduction

As we all know, that we can create Fiori charts easily using the standard templates provided by SAP, but it does not give enough flexibility to customize on top of that, when we want to make any changes in those standard templates then we must make use of custom charts. For custom charts, VizFrames are used to perform the customization on the chart as per the requirement.

In one of my recent implementations, I defined the custom chart using VizFrame with two lines and two columns on x and y axis. Apart from this there was a dynamic colour change option given to the user, so that they can dynamically change colors of both the lines and columns, and the colour once picked, has persisted in the localStorage of the browser for next time use.

This blog explains the implementation steps:

Business Requirement

  • Dual combination chart (2lines and 2 bars)
  • Allow user to personalize chart colours.
  • Saves the colour preference.

Solution

To achieve the above requirement, we have used

  • sap.viz.ui5.controls.VizFrame
  • dual_combinatoin chart type
  • ColorPickerPopover
  • Sap.m.Menu
  • Browser localStorage

Step1: Create a dual combination chart using VizFrame:

The custom chart is created using VizFrame with dual_combination as the chart type.

<viz:VizFrame id="idVizFrame" vizType="dual_combination" uiConfig="{applicationSet:'fiori'}">
</viz:VizFrame>

Step2: Define the chart dimensions and measures:

Here, I am defining the chart dimensions and measures.

Dimension:<vizDataset:DimensionDefinition
                             name="Period"
                             value="{Date}" />

Measure:  <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric A"
                              value="{Value1}" />

                     <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric B"
                             value="{Value2}" />

                   <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric C"
                             value="{Value3}" />

                 <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric D"
                             value="{Value4}" />

Step3: Configuring chart feeds:

First, I have configured the primary axis to display column measures and then the secondary axis to display line measures.

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="valueAxis" type="Measure" values="Metric A,Metric B"/>

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="valueAxis2" type="Measure" values="Metric C,Metric D"/>

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="categoryAxis" type="Dimension" values="Period"/>

Step4: Creating colour palette button:

Enabling palette button as drop-down menu, so that the user can select the measure for which personalized colour needs to be picked.

<Button
       icon="sap-icon://palette"
       type="Transparent"
       tooltip="Change Chart Colors"
       press=".onOpenColorSettings"/>

When the user clicks on the menu item, a color selection menu appears.

onOpenColorSettings: function(oEvent) {
        if (!this._oColorMenu) {
            this._oColorMenu = new sap.m.Menu({
                items: [
                   new sap.m.MenuItem({ 
                   text: "Metric A",
                   press: this.onMeasureSelect.bind(this, "Metric A")   }),
                   new sap.m.MenuItem({
                   text: "Metric B",
                   press: this.onMeasureSelect.bind(this, "Metric B")  })
               ]
         });
      }
     this.oColorMenu.openBy(oEvent.getSource());
    }

As shown in the snap below, we get a colour palette button, with the dropdown menu and all the required options, and when we select on each one, we get the colour picker as shown in the snap below.

     

Step5: Define the default colours during Initialization:

I have set the default colour values for all measures inside onInit() method.

this.aColors = {
     "Metric A": "#2E86AB",
     "Metric B": "#28B463",
     "Metric C": "#F39C12",
     "Metric D": "#8E44AD"
 };

Step6: Applying personalized colours using dataPointStyle in VizFrame:

I have used VizFrame's dataPointStyle config to apply personalized colours to the measures.

plotArea: {
     dataPointStyle: {
          rules: [
            { 
              dataContext: { measureNames: "Metric A"},
               properties: {color: "#2E86AB"}
            },
           {
               dataContext: {measureNames: "Metric B"},
               properties: {color: "#28B463"}
           }
         ]

Step7: Open color picker when the measure is selected:

When the measure is selected from the menu, a color picker dialog appears.

_openColorPicker: function(oControl, sMeasure) {

        if (!this._oColorPicker) {
             this._oColorPicker = new sap.ui.unified.ColorPickerPopover({
                  colorString: "HEX",
                  change: fuction(oEvent) {
                       var sColor = oEvent.getParameter("colorString");
                        this.applyMeasureColor( sMeasure, sColor );
                  }.bind(this)
             });
           }
       this._oColorPicker.openBy(oControl);
      }

Step8: To dynamically update the VizFrame properties:

The selected color is updated dynamically by updating the VizFrame properties.

_applyMeasureColor: function(sMeasure, sColor) {
          var oVizFrame = this.byId("idVizFrame");
          var oProps = oVizFrame.getVizProperties();
          oProps.plotArea.dataPointStyle.rules =
                oProps.plotArea.dataPointStyle.rules.map(function(oRule) {
                      if (oRule.dataContext.measureNames === sMeasure) {
                               oRule.properties.color = sColor;
                        }
                   return oRule;
                });
               oVizFrame.setVizProperties(oProps);
    }

Step9: Persist the selected colours using browser local storage:

The user selected colors are stored in the browser's local storage, so that the preferred colors are picked up when you reopen the application.

localStorage.setItem(
        "CHART_COLORS",
          JSON.stringify(oColorObject)
    );

We can retrieve the same during initialization, so that the colours remain consistent even while reopening the application.
 

 JSON.parse(
        localStorage.getItem("CHART_COLORS")
   );

Output

Here is the final working chart snap:

Conclusion

This shows that instead of limiting to pre-defined charts or templates, one can personalize the charts and visualize their own preferences and in turn makes it easy to read and analyse data using VizFrame.

Reference: Documentation - Demo Kit - SAPUI5 SDK

entry.title: RAP Augmentation in Managed Scenario – Enriching Transactional Requests Before BO Processing
entry.content:
Introduction

The RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides several extension points for implementing business logic, such as Determinations, Validations, Actions, and Prechecks. RAP Augmentation adds another extension point that allows developers to enrich incoming create or update requests before the framework starts processing the business object.

This early execution makes augmentation useful for scenarios where default values, audit information, or business-specific data must be available before any further RAP processing takes place. In this blog, we will understand RAP Augmentation, compare it with Determinations, and implement it using an Employee Management example.

Business Requirement

I had a requirement  an Employee Management application where users create and maintain employee records. During record creation, the application should automatically populate business-specific information instead of relying on users to enter every value manually.

The application should:

  • Set the employee status to PENDING_APPROVAL.
  • Determine whether approval is required based on the selected department.
  • Capture the current user as the creator.
  • Maintain audit information during updates.

Instead of implementing all this logic inside the business object, we will use RAP Augmentation to enrich the incoming request before standard processing begins.

Problem Statement

Determinations are commonly used in RAP to derive or update field values. However, they execute after the request has already entered the transactional buffer.

In some business scenarios, values must be available even before the business object starts processing. For example, default values or audit fields may be required before validations or other business logic execute. In such cases, Augmentation provides a cleaner and more suitable solution by enriching the request at the beginning of the transaction.

What is RAP Augmentation?

RAP Augmentation is an extension mechanism that runs during the Interactive Phase of a RAP transaction. It allows developers to enrich incoming create or update requests before the framework processes the business object.

Rather than modifying persisted data, augmentation focuses on preparing the request by supplying additional values or applying business-specific logic. Once the request is enriched, RAP continues with its normal processing flow.

Augmentation Flow

The request passes through augmentation before reaching the business object implementation. During this stage, additional values can be added or modified, ensuring the business object receives a complete request for further processing.

The overall execution flow is:

  • User sends a create or update request.
  • RAP invokes the registered augmentation.
  • The request is enriched with additional data.
  • Standard RAP processing continues.
  • Data is persisted to the database.

Augmentation vs Determination

Defined at the Projection Behavior Definition using use create/update (augment).Defined at the Interface Behavior Definition using  determination.
Implemented in a Projection Behavior Implementation Class Implemented in the Behavior Pool  class.
Runs during the Interactive Phase, before the BO implementation processes the request.Runs during ON MODIFY or ON SAVE, after data enters the transactional buffer.
Used to enrich or modify the incoming request before BO processing.Used to derive or recalculate values based on data in the transactional buffer.

When Execution timing matter?

The main advantage of Augmentation is when it executes. Since it runs before business object processing, any values added during augmentation are available to the rest of the RAP processing flow.

  • If the application should automatically set an employee's status or determine whether approval is required, these values are already present when the framework performs subsequent processing. If the same logic were implemented in a Determination, the values would only be available later in the transaction.

When to Use Augmentation?

Augmentation is a good choice when the incoming request needs to be enriched before RAP starts processing the business object.

Typical use cases include:

  • Setting default values.
  • Populating audit fields.
  • Deriving values from user input.
  • Performing lightweight request enrichment.
  • Preparing data required by later processing steps.

It is not intended to replace Determinations, which remain the preferred option for business logic that should execute after the request has entered the transactional buffer.

 

BDEF of Interface

managed implementation in class zbp_aky_i_emp unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for zaky_i_emp //alias <alias_name>
persistent table zaky_t_emp

lock master

authorization master ( instance )
//etag master <field_name>
{
  create( authorization : global ) ;
  update;
  delete;
  action Approve result [1] $self;
  mapping for zaky_t_emp
{
  Employeeid        = employeeid;
  Firstname         = firstname;
  Lastname          = lastname;
  Department        = department;
  Status            = status;
  Approvalrequired  = approvalrequired;
  Createdby         = createdby;
  Createdat         = createdat;
  Lastchangedby     = lastchangedby;
  Lastchangedat     = lastchangedat;
  }
}

BDEF of Projection

projection implementation in class zbp_aky_c_emp unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for zaky_c_emp //alias <alias_name>
{
  use create( augment );
  use update( augment );
    use delete;
  field ( readonly ) Status;
  field ( readonly ) Approvalrequired;
  field ( readonly ) Createdby;
  field ( readonly ) Createdat;
  field ( readonly ) Lastchangedby;
  field ( readonly ) Lastchangedat;
  use action Approve;
}

BP of Projection

CLASS lhc_zaky_c_emp DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS augment_create FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING entities FOR CREATE zaky_c_emp.

    METHODS augment_update FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING entities FOR UPDATE zaky_c_emp.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_zaky_c_emp IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD augment_create.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).

    IF entity-Department IS INITIAL.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %cid = entity-%cid
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
                 severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                 text     = 'Department is mandatory' ) )
      TO reported-zaky_c_emp.        

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = entity-%cid )
      TO failed-zaky_c_emp.          
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.

    MODIFY AUGMENTING ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp  
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      CREATE FIELDS ( Status Approvalrequired Createdby Createdat )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        %cid             = entity-%cid
        Status           = 'PENDING_APPROVAL'
        Approvalrequired = COND #(
                             WHEN entity-Department = 'HR'
                               OR entity-Department = 'FIN'
                             THEN abap_true
                             ELSE abap_false )
        Createdby        = sy-uname
        Createdat        = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  )
      ) ).

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD augment_update.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).

    MODIFY AUGMENTING ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp   
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      UPDATE FIELDS ( Lastchangedby Lastchangedat )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        Employeeid    = entity-Employeeid
        Lastchangedby = sy-uname
        Lastchangedat = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  )
      ) ).

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

BP of interface

METHOD Approve.

  " Read current status of the employee
  READ ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zaky_i_emp
    FIELDS ( Status Employeeid )
    WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(employees)
    FAILED DATA(read_failed).

  " Loop and update status
  LOOP AT employees INTO DATA(employee).

    " Only approve if current status is PENDING_APPROVAL
    IF employee-Status <> 'PENDING_APPROVAL'.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %tky = employee-%tky
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
                 severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                 text     = 'Only records with PENDING_APPROVAL status can be approved' ) )
      TO reported-zaky_i_emp.
      APPEND VALUE #( %tky = employee-%tky )
      TO failed-zaky_i_emp.
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.

    " Update status to APPROVED
    MODIFY ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      UPDATE FIELDS ( Status )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        %tky   = employee-%tky
        Status = 'APPROVED'
      ) )
      FAILED   DATA(update_failed)
      REPORTED DATA(update_reported).

    " Pass back result
    APPEND VALUE #(
      %tky   = employee-%tky
      %param = employee
    ) TO result.

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Output

Click on the create 

After adding the value click on the create and put the debugger on the create-augment method to check it is triggering or not because we are doing managed application where creation is handled by framework

Execute

Currently it is in pending state click on the Approve employee.

After clicking on action

If you not adding the department

 

Conclusion

RAP Augmentation provides a simple and effective way to enrich transactional requests before the RAP framework begins processing the business object. It is particularly useful for populating default values, maintaining audit information, and preparing request data that should be available throughout the transaction.

In this example, we used augmentation to automatically populate employee details while keeping the business object implementation clean and maintainable. Understanding when to use Augmentation instead of Determinations helps developers choose the right extension point and build more maintainable RAP applications

entry.title: Trigger SAP Build Process Automation Workflow from Fiori Application
entry.content:
In this blog, we will learn how to trigger an SAP Build Process Automation (BPA) workflow directly from a SAPUI5/Fiori application.

This approach is useful when users need to start approval processes, requests, or business workflows directly from the application UI.

Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure the following things are done:

  1. Cloud Identity Services must be subscribed.
  2. SAP Build Process Automation must be subscribed.
  3. SAP Build Work Zone must be subscribed.
  4. Workflow must be deployed successfully.
  5. User must have the role: “ProcessAutomationParticipant”

Note: Workflow triggering from the UI will work after deployment because BPA runtime is accessed through destinations configured in SAP BTP.

You can find the complete implementation here: https://github.com/MaheshSirsat/trigger-bpa-workflow-from-fiori/tree/main 

Step 1 – Fetch SAP Build Process Automation Credentials

We need runtime credentials from the SAP Build Process Automation instance. These credentials will be used while creating the destination.

Navigation

  1. Open SAP BTP Cockpit.
  2. Go to Instances and Subscriptions.
  3. Locate your SAP Build Process Automation instance.

 

  1. Open the instance.
  2. Click View Credentials.

 

 

The credentials contain:

  • API endpoint URL
  • OAuth client ID
  • Client secret
  • Token URL

These details allow our Fiori application to communicate securely with the BPA runtime.

Step 2 – Create Destination

We create a destination that acts as a secure bridge between the Fiori application and the SAP Build Process Automation APIs.

Navigation

  1. Open Destinations
  2. Click Create Destination

 

 

 

Configuration

Name : sap_build_process_automation

URL : endpoints.api

Authentication : OAuth2JWTBearer

Client ID : uaa.clientid

Client Secret : uaa.clientsecret

Token Service URL : uaa.url/oauth/token

Additional Properties

 

endpoints : <copy complete endpoints object>

html5-apps-repo : <copy complete html5-apps-repo object>

saasregistryenabled : true

sap.cloud.service : com.sap.spa.processautomation

sap.cloud.service.alias : spa

The destination stores all connectivity information required to call BPA APIs. Instead of hardcoding credentials inside the application, SAP BTP securely manages them through the destination.

Step 3 – Create Fiori Application

Create a SAPUI5/Fiori application that will contain a button to start the workflow.

You can create:

  • Freestyle Application
  • List Report Application

After creation, run the application locally and ensure it opens successfully.

 

Step 4 – Configure xs-app.json

Add the following route inside xs-app.json:

{

  "source": "^/workflow/(.*)$",

  "target": "/public/workflow/rest/$1",

  "destination": "sap_build_process_automation",

  "authenticationType": "xsuaa",

  "csrfProtection": false

}

 

Whenever the application calls:

/workflow/*

the App Router automatically forwards the request to the destination configured earlier.

Step 5 – Create Workflow Trigger Button

Add a button in the UI:

<mvc:View
    controllerName="project2.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m">
    <Page title="Workflow Demo">
        <Button  text="Start WF"  press="onstartWF"/>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

 

The button allows users to start the workflow directly from the UI.

Step 6 – Implement Workflow Trigger Logic

Create the following function inside the controller:

   onstartWF: async function () {
            var data = {
                definitionId: "myworkflow", // workflow-defination-id
                context: { "name": "mahesh" } // your context
            };
            await $.ajax({
                url: this._getWorkflowRuntimeBaseURL() + "/workflow-instances",
                method: "POST",
                async: false,
                contentType: "application/json",

                data: JSON.stringify(data),
                success: function (result, xhr, data) {
                    sap.m.MessageToast.show('Workflow started successfully...')
                },
                error: function (request, status, error) {
                    console.error('Error in Trigger workflow')
                }
            });
        },

 

This code sends a POST request to the BPA runtime API and creates a new workflow instance.

Understanding the Payload

{
    definitionId: "myworkflow",
    context: {        "name": "mahesh"    }
}

definitionId

definitionId: "myworkflow"

This is the workflow definition ID of the deployed workflow.

 

BPA uses this value to identify which workflow should be started.

context

context: {

    "name": "mahesh"

}

This data becomes the workflow context and can be accessed inside forms, approvals, conditions, and automation steps.

Step 7 – Generate Runtime URL

Create the helper method:

_getWorkflowRuntimeBaseURL: function () {
    var appId =this.getOwnerComponent().getManifestEntry("/sap.app/id");
    var appPath =appId.replaceAll(".", "/");
    var appModulePath = jQuery.sap.getModulePath(appPath);
    return appModulePath + "/workflow/v1";
}

 

Instead of hardcoding BPA URLs, the application uses the App Router route configured in xs-app.json.

 

Step 8 – Build and Deploy Application

Build the application:

Deploy it to SAP BTP.

 

The workflow runtime destination and App Router configuration are available only after deployment.

Step 9 – Create Catalog, Group, and Role in Work Zone

After deployment:

  1. Create Catalog
  2. Create Group
  3. Create Role
  4. Assign application tile
  5. Assign role to user

Step 10 – Test from Launchpad

  1. Open SAP Build Work Zone.
  2. Launch the application.
  3. Click Start WF.

Expected result:

Workflow started successfully...

 

You can verify the workflow instance in SAP Build Process Automation Monitoring.

Final Result

We have successfully triggered an SAP Build Process Automation workflow directly from a Fiori application.

✔ BPA credentials are retrieved from the BPA instance.

✔ Destination securely manages authentication and connectivity.

✔ xs-app.json routes requests through App Router.

✔ UI button initiates workflow execution.

✔ Workflow context can be passed dynamically from the application.

✔ Users can start workflows directly from SAP Build Work Zone.

entry.title: Visualizing Business Processes in SAPUI5 using the sap.suite.ui.commons.ProcessFlow Control
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

In this blog post I'm going to explain the ProcessFlow control in SAPUI5 in a simple way, with the steps I followed while trying it out.

Overview:

So, in simple words, ProcessFlow is a control which is used to show a chain of connected documents as one single picture, instead of showing them as separate rows in a table.

For example, in most SAP scenarios one document leads to another document. A Sales Order leads to a Delivery, and the Delivery leads to an Invoice. If we show these in a normal list, all three will appear as separate rows, and the user cannot easily tell how they are connected or which one is stuck. So, by using the ProcessFlow control, we can show all three documents together in one single flow, with colors showing the status of each document.

So here I have created one small project to try this out, where I have taken a simple Order-to-Cash example - Sales Order, Delivery, and Invoice - and shown them using the ProcessFlow control.

Why I used ProcessFlow instead of building it myself

So, first I thought of building this on my own using some divs and CSS and manually giving colors based on status. But then I realized SAPUI5 already gives a standard control for this, so I did not have to build the status colors, click behaviour, or zoom behaviour on my own. Also, since it is a standard Fiori control, it looks and behaves the same as other Fiori apps, so I did not have to do any extra styling.

Project Structure 

The important files for this control are inside the webapp folder, mainly the view, controller, model, and manifest.json file, same as any normal SAPUI5 app.

processflowcontrol/ 
├── webapp/ 
│   ├── controller/ 
│   │   ├── App.controller.js 
│   │   └── View1.controller.js 
│   ├── css/ 
│   ├── i18n/ 
│   ├── model/ 
│   │   ├── data.json  
│   │   └── models.js 
│   ├── test/ 
│   ├── view/ 
│   │   ├── App.view.xml 
│   │   └── View1.view.xml 
│   ├── Component.js 
│   ├── index.html 
│   └── manifest.json 
├── package.json 
└── README.md

Step 1: Declaring the Data Model in manifest.json 

So, first we have to declare our JSON model in the manifest.json file, so that the framework will load it automatically when the app starts. We don't need to write any extra code in the controller for this.

"models": { 
 "i18n": { 
   "type": "sap.ui.model.resource.ResourceModel", 
   "settings": { 
     "bundleName": "processflowcontrol.i18n.i18n" 
   } 
 }, 
 "process": { 
   "type": "sap.ui.model.json.JSONModel", 
   "uri": "model/data.json" 
 } 
} 

Step 2: Create the JSON data (model/data.json)

So, the ProcessFlow control needs two things in the JSON file - lanes and nodes.

lanes means the stages, like Sales Order, Delivery, and Invoice. nodes means the actual documents which sit inside these stages.

{ 
 "lanes": [ 
   { "id": "order",    "label": "Sales Order", "icon": "sap-icon://sales-order",     "position": 0 }, 
   { "id": "delivery", "label": "Delivery",     "icon": "sap-icon://shipping-status", "position": 1 }, 
   { "id": "invoice",  "label": "Invoice",      "icon": "sap-icon://receipt",         "position": 2 } 
 ], 
 "nodes": [ 
   { "id": "SO-1001",  "lane": "order",    "title": "SO 1001",       "abbr": "SO", "state": "Positive", "stateText": "Completed", "children": ["DEL-2001"] }, 
   { "id": "DEL-2001", "lane": "delivery", "title": "Delivery 2001", "abbr": "DE", "state": "Positive", "stateText": "Shipped",   "children": ["INV-3001"] }, 
   { "id": "INV-3001", "lane": "invoice",  "title": "Invoice 3001",  "abbr": "IN", "state": "Critical", "stateText": "Overdue",   "children": null } 
 ] 
}

 

A few important points about the JSON data:

  • position (on lanes) — controls the left-to-right order of the lanes. I removed it once in my own testing file just to check, and the Delivery lane came before the Sales Order lane. So this field decides the order, and it's easy to forget.

  • children (on nodes) — this is what draws the arrow to the next node. SO-1001 has DEL-2001 as its child, so an arrow is drawn from SO-1001 to DEL-2001, and so on. Since children is written as an array, we can give more than one document ID here if the process branches into two paths.

  • state (on nodes) — decides the color of the node:

    • Positive → green

    • Critical → orange

    • Negative → red

    • Neutral → grey

  • stateText (on nodes) — just the small label shown under the node (like "Completed" or "Overdue").

Step 3: Bind the control in the view (View1.view.xml)

<mvc:View controllerName="processflowcontrol.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:n="sap.suite.ui.commons">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <Panel headerText="Order Fulfillment Status">
            <n:ProcessFlow id="orderProcessFlow" scrollable="false" nodes="{process>/nodes}" lanes="{process>/lanes}" nodePress="onNodePressed">
                <n:nodes>
                    <n:ProcessFlowNode laneId="{process>lane}" nodeId="{process>id}" title="{process>title}" titleAbbreviation="{process>abbr}" children="{process>children}" state="{process>state}" stateText="{process>stateText}" highlighted="{process>highlighted}" />
                </n:nodes>
                <n:lanes>
                    <n:ProcessFlowLaneHeader laneId="{process>id}" text="{process>label}" iconsrc="{process>icon}" position="{process>position}" />
                </n:lanes>
            </n:ProcessFlow>
        </Panel>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

So here we are simply binding the nodes and lanes arrays from our JSON model to the ProcessFlow control, and inside that giving the aggregation binding for ProcessFlowNode and ProcessFlowLaneHeader.

Step 4: Handling Node Clicks (View1.controller.js) 

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], (Controller, MessageToast) => {
    "use strict";
    return Controller.extend("processflowcontrol.controller.View1", {
        onInit() {
        },
        onNodePressed: function (oEvent) {
            var oNode = oEvent.getSource();
            var sTitle = oNode.getTitle();
            MessageToast.show("Node selected: " + sTitle);
        }
    });
});

So, in the above code, whenever a user clicks on any node, the onNodePressed function will be called, and we get the clicked node using oEvent.getSource(). From there we can read its title, and any other property we need. I was a little confused initially whether to read the node details from getSource() or from the event parameters, but getSource() gave me the correct node object, so I used that.

Here I have just shown a MessageToast with the node title when clicked, but in an actual application, this is the place where we can navigate to the object page of that particular document.

Output

Conclusion:

So, this is how we can use the ProcessFlow control in SAPUI5 to show a chain of connected documents in a single, easy-to-understand picture. Instead of showing Sales Order, Delivery, and Invoice as separate rows in a table, we can show them together as one flow, with colors telling us the status of each document at a glance.We just need to give the control two things - lanes (the stages) and nodes (the documents) - in a simple JSON format, and the control takes care of the rest: drawing the lanes in order, placing the nodes correctly, drawing the arrows between them, coloring them based on status, and also giving us zoom and click functionality without any extra code.This same JSON structure is not limited to Sales Order-Delivery-Invoice - we can reuse it for any multi-step process, like a support ticket lifecycle, a purchase approval chain, or an onboarding process, just by changing the lane and node data.So this is a simple and quick way to build a document flow screen without writing our own timeline UI from scratch.

entry.title: Injecting Live KPI Tiles into a Fiori Elements V2 List Report Page
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Introduction

The SAP Fiori Elements V2 List Report is a standard, highly efficient floorplan for displaying tabular data with built-in filtering, sorting, and object page navigation. However, a common business requirement is to display high-level analytical metrics — a dashboard-style KPI tile area — directly above the SmartFilterBar. Out of the box, Fiori Elements V2 does not provide a native configuration for this.

This guide covers a complete, working implementation to programmatically inject three live KPI tiles into a Fiori Elements V2 List Report header using the following:

  • sap.suite.ui.generic.template (V2 Smart Templates)
  • Northwind OData V2 service via a local proxy
  • sap.m.GenericTile with NumericContent
  • V2 Controller Extension (plain-object pattern) for safe Control Tree injection and data fetching

 

Why I Wrote This

Recently, I was tasked with adding a "dashboard" feel to a standard Fiori Elements List Report. The moment I started researching the best way to implement KPI tiles above the filter bar, I realized there wasn't a clear, end-to-end guide for V2 Smart Templates.

Most documentation either pointed towards V4 ControllerExtension patterns (which crash V2 apps), relied on Analytical List Pages (which is a different floorplan entirely), or suggested using the SmartFilterBarControlConfigurationExtension (which breaks the UI layout).

This post walks through the complete, robust flow: extending the V2 controller, safely injecting the tiles into the UI5 Control Tree (without touching the DOM), fetching aggregate counts efficiently using the standard OData model, and linking those tiles to custom interactive fragments.

 

First, Let's Understand the Concept—The Factory Car

Before jumping into code, here is how I approach customizing Fiori Elements.

Picture a Fiori Elements app as a factory-built car. It comes pre-assembled, highly optimized, and runs perfectly. If we want to add a custom hood ornament (our KPI tiles), we can't just bolt it on anywhere, it will damage the engine (the framework).

Instead of trying to force standard XML view extensions into slots that aren't built for layouts, we use JavaScript to gently place our tiles into the DynamicPageHeader right after the view renders. This keeps the factory engine untouched while giving the users the custom dashboard they want.

 

Application Overview

The setup relies on standard SAPUI5 controls injected cleanly into the generated view. Here is the folder structure I used:

project-root/
│
├── webapp/
│   ├── annotations/
│   │   └── annotation.xml
│   ├── ext/
│   │   ├── controller/
│   │   │   └── CustomTilesController.controller.js   ← Tile injection & logic
│   │   └── fragment/
│   │       ├── CustomersDialog.fragment.xml          ← Customers popup
│   │       └── ProductsDialog.fragment.xml           ← Products popup
│   ├── i18n/
│   ├── localService/mainService/
│   │   └── metadata.xml
│   ├── Component.js
│   ├── index.html
│   └── manifest.json

 

Step 1: Configuring manifest.json

To inject our custom logic, we only need a single controller extension entry. We do not need an sap.ui.viewExtensions entry because we will inject the tiles programmatically.

Add the following to the sap.ui5 -> extends -> extensions block in manifest.json:

"sap.ui5": {
  "extends": {
    "extensions": {
      "sap.ui.controllerExtensions": {
        "sap.suite.ui.generic.template.ListReport.view.ListReport": {
          "controllerName": "tilesinlistreport.ext.controller.CustomTilesController"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

 

Step 2: The Controller Extension (Injection & Logic)

Create a controller file at webapp/ext/controller/CustomTilesController.controller.js .

This is the core of the solution. It builds the tiles, waits for the standard Fiori Elements view to render, finds the DynamicPageHeader, and injects our layout. Importantly, it uses the standard OData V2 model already attached to the view to fetch aggregate data, avoiding CORS issues and native fetch() errors.

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/m/GenericTile",
    "sap/m/TileContent",
    "sap/m/NumericContent",
    "sap/ui/layout/HorizontalLayout",
    "sap/ui/model/Filter",
    "sap/ui/model/FilterOperator",
    "sap/m/MessageToast",
    "sap/ui/core/Fragment"
], function (GenericTile, TileContent, NumericContent, HorizontalLayout,
             Filter, FilterOperator, MessageToast, Fragment) {
    "use strict";

    return {
        // Called by V2 Smart Template after the view renders
        onAfterRendering: function () {
            if (!this._bTilesInjected) {
                this._bTilesInjected = true;
                this._injectTiles();
            }
        },

        // Build and inject tile layout into the DynamicPageHeader
        _injectTiles: function () {
            var oView = this.getView();

            // Store NumericContent refs for value updates after data fetch
            this._oNumTotalOrders = new NumericContent({ value: "...", valueColor: "Good",    indicator: "None", withMargin: false });
            this._oNumProducts    = new NumericContent({ value: "...", valueColor: "Neutral", indicator: "None", withMargin: false });
            this._oNumCustomers   = new NumericContent({ value: "...", valueColor: "Neutral", indicator: "None", withMargin: false });

            var oLayout = new HorizontalLayout({
                allowWrapping: true,
                content: [
                    this._makeTile("Total Orders",    "All records",  "Orders",    this._oNumTotalOrders, this._onTotalOrdersPress.bind(this)),
                    this._makeTile("Total Products",  "In catalogue", "Products",  this._oNumProducts,    this._onProductsPress.bind(this)),
                    this._makeTile("Total Customers", "Registered",   "Customers", this._oNumCustomers,   this._onCustomersPress.bind(this))
                ]
            }).addStyleClass("sapUiSmallMarginTop sapUiSmallMarginBottom sapUiSmallMarginBeginEnd");

            // Primary strategy: Inject into DynamicPageHeader
            var aAll = oView.findAggregatedObjects(true);
            var oDynamicPage = null;
            var oSFB = null;

            for (var i = 0; i < aAll.length; i++) {
                var sName = aAll[i].getMetadata().getName();
                if (sName === "sap.f.DynamicPage") { oDynamicPage = aAll[i]; }
                if (sName === "sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar.SmartFilterBar") { oSFB = aAll[i]; }
            }

            if (oDynamicPage && oDynamicPage.getHeader()) {
                oDynamicPage.getHeader().addContent(oLayout);
                this._fetchAllTileData();
                return;
            }

            // Fallback strategy: Inject via UI5 Control Tree (NO DOM MANIPULATION)
            if (oSFB) {
                var oParent = oSFB.getParent();
                if (oParent && typeof oParent.insertContent === "function") {
                    oParent.insertContent(oLayout, 0); // Insert above the SmartFilterBar
                    this._fetchAllTileData();
                } else if (oParent && typeof oParent.insertItem === "function") {
                    oParent.insertItem(oLayout, 0);
                    this._fetchAllTileData();
                }
            }
        },

        // GenericTile factory — MUST use tileContent aggregation in constructor
        _makeTile: function (sHeader, sSubheader, sUnit, oNumericContent, fnPress) {
            var oTile = new GenericTile({
                header: sHeader,
                subheader: sSubheader,
                frameType: "TwoByOne",
                tileContent: [
                    new TileContent({ unit: sUnit, content: oNumericContent })
                ]
            });
            oTile.addStyleClass("sapUiTinyMarginEnd");
            if (fnPress) { oTile.attachPress(fnPress); }
            return oTile;
        },

        // ── Press Handlers ────────────────────────────────────────────────

        _onTotalOrdersPress: function () {
            var oSFB = this._getSmartFilterBar();
            if (oSFB) { oSFB.clear(); oSFB.search(); }
            var sCount = this._oNumTotalOrders.getValue();
            MessageToast.show("Showing all " + sCount + " orders");
        },

        _onProductsPress: function () {
            var oView = this.getView();
            var sTitle = "Products (" + this._oNumProducts.getValue() + ")";
            if (!this._oProductsDialog) {
                Fragment.load({
                    id: oView.getId() + "-products",
                    name: "tilesinlistreport.ext.fragment.ProductsDialog",
                    controller: this
                }).then(function (oDialog) {
                    this._oProductsDialog = oDialog;
                    oView.addDependent(oDialog);
                    oDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                    oDialog.open();
                }.bind(this));
            } else {
                this._oProductsDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                this._oProductsDialog.open();
            }
        },

        onProductsDialogClose: function () {
            if (this._oProductsDialog) { this._oProductsDialog.close(); }
        },

        onProductSearch: function (oEvent) {
            var sQuery = oEvent.getParameter("newValue") || oEvent.getParameter("query") || "";
            var oTable = sap.ui.core.Fragment.byId(this.getView().getId() + "-products", "productsTable");
            if (oTable) {
                var oBinding = oTable.getBinding("items");
                oBinding.filter(sQuery ? [new Filter([
                    new Filter("ProductName", FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery)
                ], false)] : []);
            }
        },

        _onCustomersPress: function () {
            var oView = this.getView();
            var sTitle = "Customers (" + this._oNumCustomers.getValue() + ")";
            if (!this._oCustomersDialog) {
                Fragment.load({
                    id: oView.getId() + "-customers",
                    name: "tilesinlistreport.ext.fragment.CustomersDialog",
                    controller: this
                }).then(function (oDialog) {
                    this._oCustomersDialog = oDialog;
                    oView.addDependent(oDialog);
                    oDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                    oDialog.open();
                }.bind(this));
            } else {
                this._oCustomersDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                this._oCustomersDialog.open();
            }
        },

        onCustomersDialogClose: function () {
            if (this._oCustomersDialog) { this._oCustomersDialog.close(); }
        },

        onCustomerSearch: function (oEvent) {
            var sQuery = oEvent.getParameter("newValue") || oEvent.getParameter("query") || "";
            var oTable = sap.ui.core.Fragment.byId(this.getView().getId() + "-customers", "customersTable");
            if (oTable) {
                var oBinding = oTable.getBinding("items");
                oBinding.filter(sQuery ? [new Filter([
                    new Filter("CompanyName",  FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("ContactName",  FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("CustomerID",   FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("City",         FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("Country",      FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery)
                ], false)] : []);
            }
        },

        // ── Helpers & Data Fetching ───────────────────────────────────────

        _getSmartFilterBar: function () {
            var aAll = this.getView().findAggregatedObjects(true);
            for (var i = 0; i < aAll.length; i++) {
                if (aAll[i].getMetadata().getName() === "sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar.SmartFilterBar")
                    return aAll[i];
            }
            return null;
        },

        _fetchAllTileData: function () {
            // Using the existing standard OData V2 Model attached to the view.
            var oModel = this.getView().getModel();
            var that = this;

            var fnGetCount = function (sPath) {
                return new Promise(function (resolve) {
                    oModel.read(sPath, {
                        urlParameters: { "$top": 0, "$inlinecount": "allpages" },
                        success: function (oData) {
                            var iCount = (oData && oData.__count) ? parseInt(oData.__count, 10) : 0;
                            resolve(iCount);
                        },
                        error: function () {
                            resolve(0); // Fail gracefully
                        }
                    });
                });
            };

            Promise.all([
                fnGetCount("/Orders"),
                fnGetCount("/Products"),
                fnGetCount("/Customers")
            ]).then(function (aCounts) {
                that._oNumTotalOrders.setValue(String(aCounts[0]));
                that._oNumProducts.setValue(String(aCounts[1]));
                that._oNumCustomers.setValue(String(aCounts[2]));
            });
        }
    };
});

 

Step 3: Interactive Dialog Fragments

When a user interacts with the "Total Customers" or "Total Products" tiles, a searchable dialog opens. These are built using standard XML fragments binding directly to the existing OData model.

Note that the dialog titles in the fragments below are left as plain labels ("Products", "Customers") rather than hardcoded counts—the controller sets the live count on the title at runtime (see _onProductsPress / _onCustomersPress in Step 2), so the number a user sees always matches the tile above it.

Here is the setup for the Products Dialog (webapp/ext/fragment/ProductsDialog.fragment.xml)

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns="sap.m" xmlns:core="sap.ui.core">
    <Dialog id="productsDialog" title="Products" contentWidth="70%" contentHeight="60%" resizable="true" draggable="true">
        <content>
            <Table id="productsTable" items="{/Products}" growing="true" growingThreshold="20">
                <headerToolbar>
                    <Toolbar>
                        <Title text="All Products"/>
                        <ToolbarSpacer/>
                        <SearchField width="30%" search=".onProductSearch" liveChange=".onProductSearch"/>
                    </Toolbar>
                </headerToolbar>
                <columns>
                    <Column width="5rem"><Text text="ID"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Product Name"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Unit Price"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Units In Stock"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Units On Order"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Discontinued"/></Column>
                </columns>
                <items>
                    <ColumnListItem>
                        <cells>
                            <Text text="{ProductID}"/>
                            <Text text="{ProductName}"/>
                            <ObjectNumber number="{UnitPrice}" unit="USD"/>
                            <Text text="{UnitsInStock}"/>
                            <Text text="{UnitsOnOrder}"/>
                            <Text text="{Discontinued}"/>
                        </cells>
                    </ColumnListItem>
                </items>
            </Table>
        </content>
        <beginButton>
            <Button text="Close" press=".onProductsDialogClose"/>
        </beginButton>
    </Dialog>
</core:FragmentDefinition>

 

Here is the setup for the Customers Dialog (webapp/ext/fragment/CustomersDialog.fragment.xml)

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns="sap.m" xmlns:core="sap.ui.core">
    <Dialog id="customersDialog" title="Customers" contentWidth="70%" contentHeight="60%" resizable="true" draggable="true">
        <content>
            <Table id="customersTable" items="{/Customers}" growing="true" growingThreshold="20">
                <headerToolbar>
                    <Toolbar>
                        <Title text="All Registered Customers"/>
                        <ToolbarSpacer/>
                        <SearchField width="30%" search=".onCustomerSearch" liveChange=".onCustomerSearch"/>
                    </Toolbar>
                </headerToolbar>
                <columns>
                    <Column width="8rem"><Text text="Customer ID"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Company Name"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Contact Name"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Contact Title"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="City"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Country"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Phone"/></Column>
                </columns>
                <items>
                    <ColumnListItem>
                        <cells>
                            <Text text="{CustomerID}"/>
                            <Text text="{CompanyName}"/>
                            <Text text="{ContactName}"/>
                            <Text text="{ContactTitle}"/>
                            <Text text="{City}"/>
                            <Text text="{Country}"/>
                            <Text text="{Phone}"/>
                        </cells>
                    </ColumnListItem>
                </items>
            </Table>
        </content>
        <beginButton>
            <Button text="Close" press=".onCustomersDialogClose"/>
        </beginButton>
    </Dialog>
</core:FragmentDefinition>

 

A Few Things to Keep in Mind

When customizing standard templates, it is incredibly easy to run into framework errors. Here are the pitfalls to watch out for:

  1. Avoid direct DOM manipulation — Never use document.createElement or parentNode.insertBefore in Fiori Elements. If the view re-renders, our DOM elements will be destroyed. Always use framework aggregations like .insertContent() or .insertItem() to safely place elements into the UI5 Control Tree.
  2. Leverage the OData model for counts — Avoid hardcoded URLs and native fetch() calls. Use oModel.read() with the URL parameters "$top": 0 and "$inlinecount": "allpages". This fetches just the count without downloading a heavy payload, and it natively handles SAP Cloud routing, BTP destinations, and security headers.
  3. Wrong extension point — Do NOT use SmartFilterBarControlConfigurationExtension for layout tiles. That slot strictly accepts sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar.ControlConfiguration elements. If we attempt to place a layout container there, the app will throw an aggregation error.
  4. Controller pattern mismatch—V2 extensions mandate a plain object return pattern (return { ... };). If we accidentally use the V4 ControllerExtension.extend() pattern, our V2 app will crash immediately upon loading.
  5. Missing the .controller.js suffix—V2 Smart Templates automatically append .controller.js during module resolution. Naming our file CustomTilesController.js will cause a silent load failure.
  6. Using addContent() on GenericTile — GenericTile does not have an addContent() method; the required aggregation is tileContent.  Always define it in the constructor. Attempting to use .addContent() will throw a TypeError. 

 

Conclusion
